titleStmt.title: Letter from Edward F. Stephenson to the Local Government Board for Ireland, 19 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Edward F. Stephenson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Edward F. Stephenson, Medical Inspector, to the Local Government Board of Ireland. In the letter, Stephenson states that he has visited the Physiological and Pathological Laboratories in Queen’s University and found that the requirements of the Cruelty to Animals Act 1876 have been complied with. He found that the animals that are held there for experimentation purposes were in good condition and well housed.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers, CSO RP/1916/3716

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Copy
No. 3716 Belfast 19th January, 1916.
To the Local Government Board for
Ireland. Gentlemen, I have the honour to report that I visited
the Physiological and Pathological Laboratories
attached to the Queen's University, Belfast, under
the provisions of Section 10 of the Cruelty to
Animals Act, 1876, and found that the requirements
of the Act were complied with. The animals (rabbits and guinea pigs) kept for
experimental purposes, were in a clean and healthy
condition, and were well housed. I have the honour to be
Gentlemen,
Your obedient Servant, Edward F. Stephenson
